TKD ECONOMY: Pakistan–Afghanistan Trade Slumps as Exports Drop by More Than 50 Percent

Pakistan’s exports to Afghanistan fell sharply during the first seven months of the current fiscal year, underscoring growing trade disruptions and strained political relations between the two neighbors.

According to official figures released by the State Bank of Pakistan, exports to Afghanistan declined by more than half compared with the same period last year. 

Export revenues dropped from over $550 million to around $230 million between July and January.

The steep decline is largely attributed to the suspension of bilateral trade since early October, which has significantly curtailed formal cross-border commercial activity and weighed heavily on Pakistan’s regional export performance.

Meanwhile, Pakistan’s imports from Afghanistan also recorded a notable decrease during the same period. Import volumes fell to just over $6 million, down from more than $15 million a year earlier, reflecting a broader contraction in two-way trade flows.

The data highlight the depth of the economic impact caused by ongoing trade interruptions, with both countries experiencing reduced commercial exchanges amid continued uncertainty.

By: Mir Yar Baloch 

History has a way of repeating itself in the most explosive forms. Just as the revolt of Indian sepoys in the British Indian Army during the 1857 Rebellion fueled by cultural grievances, exploitation, and a spark of mutiny in Meerut shattered the illusion of British invincibility and set the stage for the end of colonial rule, Pakistan today faces its own terminal reckoning. That uprising, where native soldiers turned their rifles on their oppressors, exposed the fragility of an empire built on division and coercion, accelerating the path to independence. Fast-forward to 1946, when the Royal Indian Navy mutiny in Bombay a wave of strikes by Indian sailors against racial discrimination and poor conditions delivered the final blow, convincing Britain that holding India was untenable as loyalty evaporated across ranks. The result? The British Raj crumbled, unable to quell a force that had lost faith in its masters.

Today, the scenario in Pakistan is eerily identical a powder keg of ethnic rebellion igniting within the very institution meant to suppress it: the Punjabi-dominated army. Baloch, Pashtun, and Sindhi personnel, long relegated to the frontlines as expendable shields for Punjab's elite, are now in open revolt, deserting en masse and refusing to fight against their own people in Balochistan and along the Durand Line. This isn't mere discontent; it's a seismic mutiny sweeping through the army, Frontier Corps, air force, navy, and civil administration. Thousands of non-Punjabi soldiers are withdrawing their allegiance, echoing the sepoys' defiance, as they decry a regime that disowns captured comrades to evade responsibility leaving them to rot without honor or recognition.

Even Punjabis, the supposed core of the military, are fracturing: reports confirm thousands refusing deployments to Balochistan's killing fields and the volatile Durand Line, haunted by videos of disowned eight prisoners begging for acknowledgment. 

Intercepted communications reveal troops in bunkers across Quetta, Kalat, and Gwadar weeping as families urge surrender over death for a betraying high command under General Asim Munir. Over 10,000 Punjabi soldiers have been shipped back to Punjab for court-martials after defying orders, while mutinies in ethnic units force desperate redeployments from the Indian border weakening the entire apparatus. 

Resignations surge: more than 250 officers and 1,200 soldiers in recent weeks alone, capturing the raw fury: "This war is not ours; the generals feast while we bleed as orphans."This ethnic uprising is no isolated flare-up it's a coordinated withdrawal from Pakistan's crumbling federation. 

Baloch forces, with unity expelling occupiers district by district, syncing with Sindhi nationalists in a multi-front assault on military bases and Chinese proxies. In Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pashtun resistance against enforced disappearances and demographic engineering has turned the tide, leaving the army's "undeclared war" in tatters. Ground control is lost; the military clings to air power amid a security vacuum where even tribal elders fear entering their lands.

As in 1857 and 1946, when Indian forces' revolt signaled the death of British occupation, today's Baloch, Pashtun, Sindhi and increasingly Punjabi defections foretell Pakistan's unraveling. Decades of oppression, extrajudicial killings, and exploitation have backfired, birthing an irreversible fire that no amount of denial can extinguish. Afghanistan stands ready to reclaim Pashtunistan, as the federation fragments into ethnic strongholds. This is the endgame: a predatory state betrayed by its own sword, poised for a historic implosion that redraws South Asia's map forever. The echoes of empire's fall are deafening and unmistakable.

Fidayee Liaquat Zahri was a committed soldier of the war of freedom who had made the hardships of the battlefield part of his training. For him, struggle was an organized, continuous process carried out with full seriousness. In his combat approach, patience, observation, and striking at the right moment were more prominent than haste. He was skilled in long ambushes, silent movement, and advancing through difficult terrain. His steadfastness and discipline not only kept him strong but also raised the morale of those fighting alongside him. He remained firm until the end with the belief that steadfastness is the true strength in the path of freedom, and with this conviction, he carried his struggle to its conclusion.

Fidayee Liaquat Zahri was forcibly disappeared on June 9, 2021, from Khuzdar along with the young Brahui-language poet Tabish Waseem. Two years later, he was released from the enemy’s torture cells, and after some time, instead of silently succumbing to the hands of the enemy, he chose the path of armed resistance and joined the BLA. It is noteworthy that Liaquat’s cousin, Tabish Waseem, was killed on October 17, 2022, in Kharan in a fake encounter by Pakistan’s so-called Counter Terrorism Department (CTD), and his body was dumped.

Martyr Liaquat Zahri served as Patrol Command on the Nagaho front. During Operation Herof II, he was present on the Noshki front. Fighting on the front line, he made the ground difficult for the enemy and played an important role in the success of Operation Herof Phase II. By sacrificing his life on the Noshki front, martyr Liaquat Zahri upheld the great tradition of sacrifice life for the homeland.
